title: Simple 5-Ingredient Chocolate Cake with Silky Ganache Frosting

description: This chocolate cake is super easy but tastes like you spent hours on it. Just five ingredients and a luscious ganache make it moist and rich. Great for birthdays or any time you want a special treat without the fuss!

pin_variations.title: The Best Simple Chocolate Cake with Creamy Ganache Frosting
pin_variations.description: Craving chocolate? This simple cake with a creamy ganache is moist, tender, and perfect for any occasion. Save this recipe and enjoy a homemade treat soon! 🍫🎉 #cakerecipe #ganachefrosting #easychocolatecake

ingredients: 1/2 cup unsalted butter, melted and cooled
introduction: I first made this chocolate cake on a whim when I needed a quick dessert for a last-minute gathering. I was honestly surprised how rich and moist it turned out with just five main ingredients. The real magic is in the ganache frosting—it’s so silky and smooth, it feels like a fancy bakery cake but without all the fuss. Over time, I tweaked the recipe to get the crumb tender but still sturdy enough to hold that luscious ganache. I’ve found melting the butter first and letting it cool before mixing makes the batter silky, which helps the cake stay moist. Plus, folding in the dry ingredients gently keeps it light. When it’s done, you get a shiny, rich chocolate cake that’s perfect for birthdays or any time you want a no-fail, impressive dessert. It’s become a go-to when I want something that looks special but is super easy to make. Honestly, I think this cake tastes better than some of the complicated ones I’ve tried. If you love chocolate and simplicity, you’ll enjoy making this as much as eating it!

instructions.instruction: Preheat your oven to 350°F (175°C) and grease a 9-inch round cake pan with butter or non-stick spray. Line the bottom with parchment paper to ensure the cake releases easily. This step is crucial because it prevents sticking, which can ruin the cake’s appearance and texture. Preparing the pan properly means your cake will come out clean and smooth, giving you that perfect presentation without tears or crumbling.
instructions.doneness_cue: Pan is evenly greased with parchment paper securely fitted

instructions.instruction: In a large bowl, sift together the flour, cocoa powder, and sugar. Sifting removes lumps and blends the dry ingredients evenly, which helps the cake rise uniformly and results in a tender crumb. If you skip sifting, you might get small pockets of cocoa or flour that don’t mix well, leading to uneven texture.
instructions.doneness_cue: Dry ingredients are fully combined with no lumps

instructions.instruction: In another bowl, whisk the eggs lightly, then add the melted butter once it has cooled to room temperature. The cooling is important because hot butter can cook the eggs prematurely, causing the batter to curdle. Combining eggs and butter creates a rich, smooth base that helps the cake stay moist and tender.
instructions.doneness_cue: Eggs and butter mixture is smooth and slightly thickened

instructions.instruction: Slowly pour the wet ingredients into the dry ingredients, folding gently with a spatula until just combined. Overmixing can develop gluten in the flour, making the cake tough. Folding ensures you keep the batter light while evenly incorporating everything. Stop mixing as soon as you don’t see dry flour patches.
instructions.doneness_cue: Batter is smooth, slightly thick, and uniform in color

instructions.instruction: Pour the batter into the prepared pan, smoothing the top with a spatula. Tap the pan gently on the counter a couple of times to release any air bubbles trapped inside. This helps the cake bake evenly without large holes or tunnels and ensures a consistent texture throughout.
instructions.doneness_cue: Batter is evenly spread with a smooth surface

instructions.instruction: Bake in the preheated oven for 25-30 minutes. Start checking at 25 minutes by inserting a toothpick into the center; it should come out with a few moist crumbs but not wet batter. Overbaking will dry the cake, while underbaking leaves it gooey in the middle. The right bake time creates a tender crumb that holds together well.
instructions.doneness_cue: Toothpick inserted in center comes out with moist crumbs

instructions.instruction: While the cake cools, prepare the ganache. Heat the heavy cream in a small saucepan over medium heat until it just begins to simmer—don’t let it boil or it might scorch. Pour the hot cream over chopped bittersweet chocolate in a heatproof bowl. Let it sit for a minute to soften the chocolate before stirring gently until smooth and glossy. This technique ensures a silky, shiny ganache.
instructions.doneness_cue: Ganache is smooth, glossy, and pourable

instructions.instruction: Once the cake is completely cool, remove it from the pan and place it on a serving plate. Pour the ganache over the top, letting it drip down the sides naturally. Use a spatula to gently spread if needed, but keep it loose for that beautiful shine. Cooling the cake fully prevents the ganache from melting and sliding off.
instructions.doneness_cue: Ganache coats the cake evenly with a shiny finish

instructions.instruction: Allow the ganache to set for at least 30 minutes at room temperature before slicing. This resting time lets the ganache firm up slightly while staying soft and creamy. Cutting the cake too soon can cause the ganache to smudge and the slices to look messy. When ready, use a sharp knife wiped clean between cuts for perfect slices.
instructions.doneness_cue: Ganache is set but still soft and shiny

why_this_recipe_works.section: Understanding Why This Chocolate Cake Recipe Succeeds
why_this_recipe_works.content:
This chocolate cake’s success lies in its simplicity and the balance of ingredients that create a moist, tender crumb. The melted butter adds richness and helps tenderize the cake by coating flour proteins, which limits gluten formation. This results in a softer texture without sacrificing structure. The cocoa powder not only imparts deep chocolate flavor but also reacts with the sugar and eggs to enhance moisture retention.

Folding the dry ingredients into the wet gently is crucial because it prevents overworking the batter. Overmixing activates too much gluten, making the cake dense and tough rather than light and fluffy. Sifting the flour and cocoa removes lumps and aerates the dry ingredients, which helps the cake rise evenly and improves texture.

The ganache frosting is a simple emulsion of chocolate and cream. Heating the cream just to simmering temperature allows it to melt the chocolate smoothly without scorching, which can cause bitterness or graininess. Stirring gently encourages the fats and liquids to blend into a shiny, silky coating that hardens slightly as it cools but remains creamy to the bite.

Finally, proper cooling at each stage ensures the best texture and appearance. Baking to the correct doneness avoids dryness, and letting the cake cool fully before applying ganache prevents melting and sliding. Resting the ganache allows it to set to the perfect consistency for slicing, making this cake both delicious and visually appealing.

ingredient_notes.all-purpose flour: Choose a good-quality, unbleached all-purpose flour for this cake. It provides the right protein content to create structure without toughness. Avoid bread flour, which is too high in protein and will make the cake dense. Sifting the flour before mixing also helps incorporate air, contributing to a lighter crumb.
ingredient_notes.unsweetened cocoa powder: Use natural unsweetened cocoa powder for a rich chocolate flavor and deep color. Dutch-processed cocoa is milder and less acidic, which can affect the rise and flavor balance. Make sure your cocoa is fresh, as older cocoa can taste stale or bitter, which impacts the cake’s overall taste.
ingredient_notes.unsalted butter: Using unsalted butter lets you control the salt level in the recipe. Melt the butter gently and let it cool to room temperature before mixing to avoid cooking the eggs prematurely. Butter adds richness and moisture, contributing to a tender crumb and enhancing the chocolate’s flavor.
ingredient_notes.eggs: Use large eggs at room temperature for best incorporation and structure. Eggs provide moisture, richness, and act as a binder, helping the cake hold together. Cold eggs can cause the batter to seize or not mix evenly, so letting them come to room temp improves texture.
ingredient_notes.bittersweet chocolate: Choose high-quality bittersweet chocolate with at least 60% cocoa solids for the ganache. It melts smoothly and balances sweetness with a rich depth of flavor. Avoid chocolate chips with stabilizers, as they may not melt as evenly, resulting in a grainy ganache.

pro_tips.tip: Always let melted butter cool before adding to eggs.
pro_tips.reason: Hot butter can scramble the eggs, causing lumps and uneven batter texture. Cooling the butter prevents this and helps create a smooth, silky batter that bakes evenly with a tender crumb.

pro_tips.tip: Sift dry ingredients together before mixing.
pro_tips.reason: Sifting aerates the flour and cocoa powder, removing lumps and ensuring even distribution. This step helps the cake rise uniformly and produces a finer crumb structure without dense pockets.

pro_tips.tip: Fold the batter gently instead of stirring vigorously.
pro_tips.reason: Overmixing activates gluten, making the cake tough and dense. Folding preserves the air bubbles incorporated during mixing, resulting in a lighter, fluffier cake with tender texture.

pro_tips.tip: Use room temperature eggs for better mixing.
pro_tips.reason: Cold eggs don’t combine well with melted butter and dry ingredients, which can cause curdling or uneven texture. Room temperature eggs emulsify better, giving a smooth batter and consistent crumb.

pro_tips.tip: Don’t rush cooling before frosting the cake.
pro_tips.reason: Applying ganache to a warm cake causes it to melt and slide off, ruining the finish. Cool the cake completely so the ganache sets properly with a shiny, smooth surface.

pro_tips.tip: Heat cream just to simmer, not boil, for ganache.
pro_tips.reason: Boiling cream can scorch and change flavor, also causing the ganache to seize or become grainy. Gentle heating melts chocolate evenly for a glossy, silky finish.

pro_tips.tip: Let ganache rest before slicing the cake.
pro_tips.reason: Ganache firms slightly as it cools, making it easier to cut clean slices without smearing. This also enhances texture, keeping the frosting creamy but stable.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.mistake: Using hot melted butter directly on eggs
common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Many assume melted butter can be added immediately, but hot butter cooks the eggs, causing lumps.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Always let the melted butter cool to room temperature before mixing with eggs to ensure a smooth batter.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: It’s tempting to stir vigorously to combine everything quickly, but this develops gluten.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Fold the dry ingredients into wet gently until just combined to keep the cake tender and light.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Skipping sifting saves time but leads to lumps and uneven texture.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Take the extra minute to sift dry ingredients together for a smoother, more uniform crumb.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.mistake: Applying ganache on a warm cake
common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Rushing to frost before cooling causes ganache to melt and slide off.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Allow the cake to cool completely to room temperature before pouring ganache for a neat finish.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Overheating cream scorches it and can cause a grainy texture.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Heat cream just until it simmers, then pour over chocolate and stir gently for a silky ganache.

variations.name: Mocha Chocolate Cake
variations.description: Add 1 tablespoon of instant espresso powder to the dry ingredients to enhance the chocolate flavor with a subtle coffee note. This gives the cake a deeper, more complex taste without overpowering the chocolate. The texture remains moist and tender.
variations.technical_note: Be sure to dissolve espresso powder evenly to avoid gritty spots and maintain smooth batter consistency.

variations.name: Gluten-Free Chocolate Cake
variations.description: Swap all-purpose flour with a gluten-free blend designed for baking. This makes the cake suitable for gluten-sensitive eaters while keeping the crumb tender. You might notice a slightly different texture but still rich and moist.
variations.technical_note: Choose a blend with xanthan gum included for structure; otherwise, add about 1/4 teaspoon xanthan gum to the mix.

variations.name: Vegan Chocolate Cake
variations.description: Replace eggs with flax eggs (1 tablespoon ground flaxseed + 3 tablespoons water per egg) and use a plant-based butter substitute. Use dairy-free chocolate and coconut cream for the ganache to keep it vegan-friendly. Expect a slightly denser but still delicious cake.
variations.technical_note: Let flax eggs thicken for 5 minutes before mixing to ensure proper binding.

variations.name: Chocolate Raspberry Cake
variations.description: Fold fresh or frozen raspberries into the batter before baking or add a layer of raspberry jam between cake layers before ganache frosting. This adds a bright, fruity contrast to the rich chocolate and moist crumb.
variations.technical_note: If using frozen raspberries, thaw and drain excess liquid to avoid soggy batter.

variations.name: Nutty Chocolate Cake
variations.description: Stir in 1/2 cup chopped toasted walnuts or pecans into the batter for added crunch and flavor complexity. Nuts complement the chocolate and add texture, making the cake more interesting and satisfying.
variations.technical_note: Toast nuts lightly to enhance their flavor and reduce moisture before mixing.

nutrition.note: Nutritional values are estimates and may vary based on specific ingredients used.

storage_and_reheating.room_temperature: The cake can safely sit at room temperature for up to 2 days if covered loosely with a cake dome or foil to prevent drying out. Avoid leaving it uncovered as the ganache can harden and the cake crumb can become stale.
storage_and_reheating.refrigerator: Store the cake in an airtight container in the fridge for up to 5 days. The ganache will firm up more when cold, so let the cake sit at room temperature for 20-30 minutes before serving for best texture and flavor.
storage_and_reheating.freezer: Freeze the cake tightly wrapped in plastic wrap and then foil for up to 2 months. To thaw, place it in the fridge overnight and bring to room temperature before serving to restore moistness and ganache softness.
storage_and_reheating.reheating: If desired, warm individual slices gently in the microwave for 10-15 seconds to soften the ganache and bring out flavor. Avoid overheating to prevent drying the cake or melting the ganache too much.
storage_and_reheating.make_ahead_strategy: Bake the cake layers up to 2 days ahead and keep wrapped tightly at room temperature. Prepare the ganache the day of serving for best shine and texture, then frost the cooled cake just before guests arrive.

faq.question: Can I use Dutch-processed cocoa instead of natural cocoa?
faq.answer: You can, but Dutch-processed cocoa is less acidic and may affect the cake’s rise slightly. Since this recipe doesn’t use baking soda or powder, the difference is minimal, but the flavor might be a bit milder. For best results, stick to natural unsweetened cocoa.

faq.question: What if my ganache is too thick or too thin?
faq.answer: If ganache is too thick, gently warm it over a double boiler or microwave in short bursts and stir to loosen. If it’s too thin, let it cool longer at room temperature or refrigerate briefly; it will thicken as it cools. Adjust cream or chocolate ratio in future batches if needed.

faq.question: Can I make this cake gluten-free?
faq.answer: Yes, substitute with a gluten-free all-purpose flour blend that includes xanthan gum. This helps maintain structure. The texture might be slightly different but still moist and delicious. Avoid almond or coconut flour alone as they behave differently in baking.

faq.question: How do I know when the cake is fully baked?
faq.answer: Insert a toothpick into the center around 25 minutes. It should come out with moist crumbs but no wet batter. If it’s still wet, bake a few minutes longer and check frequently to avoid overbaking and drying out the cake.

faq.question: Can I use salted butter instead of unsalted?
faq.answer: Yes, but reduce or omit any added salt in the recipe. Unsalted butter allows better control over salt levels. Using salted butter may slightly alter the flavor balance but won’t ruin the cake.

faq.question: What’s the best way to store leftover cake?
faq.answer: Keep leftovers in an airtight container at room temperature for up to 2 days or refrigerated up to 5 days. Let refrigerated cake warm to room temp before serving for best taste and texture.

faq.question: Can I double the recipe to make a larger cake?
faq.answer: Yes, doubling works well. Use two 9-inch pans or a larger pan, but adjust baking time accordingly. Start checking doneness a few minutes earlier to avoid overbaking.

faq.question: Is there a way to make this cake vegan?
faq.answer: You can replace eggs with flax eggs and use plant-based butter and dairy-free chocolate and cream for the ganache. The texture will be slightly different—denser but still tasty. Let flax eggs thicken before mixing to ensure proper binding.

conclusion: I hope you enjoy making and sharing this simple chocolate cake as much as I do! Don’t forget to share your results and tweaks—you might find your own favorite twist!
